summary: Cathy O'Neil announces she's helping launch a new data journalism program at Columbia's Journalism School. The program is a one or two semester post-baccalaureate course designed to teach data skills, coding, and visualizations to people from non-technical backgrounds. While housed in the J-School, the program is open to people from various humanities fields who want to become data-competent. The program launches in May 2014 and the team is actively recruiting students and teachers.
key_points: The program targets people from non-technical and humanities backgrounds who want to work with data
It's a practical post-bacc program covering data, coding, and visualizations
Despite being at the J-School, enrollment is open beyond journalists to other humanities fields
The program was launching quickly with institutional support and scholarship funding available
money_quotes: The idea is that people from other humanities who see value in working with data can enroll in the program and emerge competent with data.
There's no time to waste, as the program starts soon and we don't even quite have a name for it.
key_insight: Data literacy should be accessible to humanities professionals beyond just journalists, bridging the gap between technical and non-technical fields.
